meta.description: Quote library for Refrain — time-tagged verses from Baring-Gould, Songs of the West

quotes.text:
Upon a Sunday morning, when Spring was in its prime,
Along the Church-lane tripping, I heard the Church-bells chime.
quotes.source: Upon a Sunday Morning (Songs of the West, No. 3)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–2. Collected from Robert Hard, South Brent. Baring-Gould notes the melody is not a genuine folk tune, but the text was collected from a singer.

quotes.text:
And we were up as soon as the day,
For to fetch the Summer home, O!
The Summer, and the May,
Now the Winter is a gone, O!
quotes.source: The Hal-an-Tow (Songs of the West, No. 24)
quotes.notes: Chorus. Helston Furry Dance, sung on May 8th annually. 'Summer home' is the ritual bringing-in of the season — the song is a spring ceremony, not a summer one.

quotes.text:
On a moon-shiny night, O! 'tis my delight,
A-catching o' moles.
quotes.source: The Mole-Catcher (Songs of the West, No. 45)
quotes.notes: Refrain, repeated throughout. Collected from Robert Hard, South Brent.

quotes.text:
The fox went out one winter night,
And prayed the moon to give him light,
For he'd many a mile to go that night,
Before he reached his den, O!
quotes.source: The Fox (Songs of the West, No. 55)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. Collected from James Parsons. Night and winter both explicit.

quotes.text:
Come all you young fellows that carry a gun,
Beware of late shooting when daylight is done;
For 'tis little you reckon what hazards you run,
I shot my true love at the setting of the sun.
quotes.source: At the Setting of the Sun (Songs of the West, No. 62)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. Collected from Samuel Fone by Mr. Sheppard, and again by Cecil Sharp from Louie Hooper and Lucy White at Hambridge, Somerset.

quotes.text:
As I walked out one May morning,
One May morning so early;
I there espied a fair pretty maid,
All on the dew so pearly.
O! 'twas sweet, sweet spring,
Merry birds did sing,
All in the morning early.
quotes.source: On a May Morning So Early (Songs of the West, No. 73)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. Morning and spring both explicit throughout.

quotes.text:
'Twas early I walked on a midsummer morning,
The fields and the meadows were deckèd and gay,
The small birds were singing, the woodlands a-ringing,
'Twas early in the morning, at breaking of day.
quotes.source: Midsummer Carol (Songs of the West, No. 89)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–4. Collected from William Aggett of Chagford, Devon, October 1890. Morning and midsummer both explicit.

quotes.text:
Tobacco is an Indian weed,
Grows green at morn, is cut down at eve.
quotes.source: Tobacco is an Indian Weed (Songs of the West, No. 95)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–2. Morn and eve both explicit. Memento mori quality — the full verse turns on decay and mortality.

quotes.text:
The golden sun is shining bright,
The dew is off the field;
To us it is our main delight,
The fork and rake to wield.
The pipe and tabor both shall play,
The viols loudly ring,
From morn till eve each summer day,
As we go hay-making.
quotes.source: The Merry Haymakers (Songs of the West, No. 109)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. 'From morn till eve each summer day' implies sustained midday/afternoon activity. Summer explicit.

quotes.text:
It is cold winter's night,
You and I must bide alone:
Whilst my pretty lad is young
And is growing.
quotes.source: The Trees They Are So High (Songs of the West, No. 4)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 5–8. Night and winter explicit.

quotes.text:
Beside the rolling ocean
One morning in the month of June,
The feathered warbling songsters
Were sweetly changing note and tune.
quotes.source: The Bonny Bunch of Roses (Songs of the West, No. 27)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–4. Morning and June explicit.

quotes.text:
As I was a walking one morning in May,
To hear the birds whistle, see lambkins at play,
I spied a fair damsel, O sweetly sang she—
'Down by the green bushes he thinks to meet me.'
quotes.source: The Green Bushes (Songs of the West, No. 43)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. Morning and May explicit.

quotes.text:
Awake, awake ye pretty maids,
And take the May-bush in,
Or 'twill be gone ere tomorrow morn,
And you'll have none within.
quotes.source: May-Day Carol (Songs of the West, No. 47)
quotes.notes: Stanza 4. Dawn-calling carol — sung at first light on May Day. Morning implicit ('ere tomorrow morn'); spring explicit.

quotes.text:
Come awaken in the morn,
When the cock upon the dunghill
Is a-blowing of his horn.
quotes.source: The Oxen Ploughing (Songs of the West, No. 57)
quotes.notes: Stanza 2, lines 2–4. Morning explicit; spring from stanza 1 ('the ploughboy does in Spring').

quotes.text:
'Twas early one morning at breaking of day,
The cocks were a crowing, the farmer did say,
Come, arise, my good fellows, arise with good will,
For your horses want something their bellies to fill.
quotes.source: Jolly Fellows That Follow the Plough (Songs of the West, No. 63)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–4. Morning explicit.

quotes.text:
One morning so gay, in the glad month of May,
When I from my cottage strayed;
As broke the ray of awakening day,
I met a pretty maid.
quotes.source: The Spotted Cow (Songs of the West, No. 74)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–4. Morning, May, and 'awakening day' all explicit.

quotes.text:
All in a morning very fair
As I rode out to take the air
I heard some to halloo clearly.
quotes.source: The Duke's Hunt (Songs of the West, No. 81)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–3. Morning explicit.

quotes.text:
As I walked out one morn betime,
To view the fields in May, Sir,
There I espied a fair sweet maid,
Among the new-mown hay, Sir.
quotes.source: Among the New-Mown Hay (Songs of the West, No. 102)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–4. Morning and May explicit.

quotes.text:
Within a garden a maiden lingered,
When soft the shades of evening fell,
Expecting, fearing,
A footstep hearing,
Her love appearing,
To say farewell.
quotes.source: Within a Garden (Songs of the West, No. 105)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. Evening explicit.

quotes.text:
For all the day long she sits in a tree,
And when the night cometh, away flies she.
quotes.source: The Owl (Songs of the West, No. 115)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 3–4. Night explicit. Stanza 2 adds morning ('The lark in the morn ascendeth on high') — considered adding both stanzas but stanza 1 alone is crisper.

quotes.text:
The lark in the morn ascendeth on high
And leaves the poor owl to sob and to sigh;
And all the day long, the owl is asleep,
While little birds blithely are singing, cheep! cheep!
quotes.source: The Owl (Songs of the West, No. 115)
quotes.notes: Stanza 2. Morning explicit. Companion to ID 131 from the same song — different time slot.

quotes.text:
By chance it was I met my love,
It did me much surprise,
Down by a shady myrtle grove,
Just as the sun did rise.
quotes.source: By Chance It Was (Songs of the West, No. 1)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. 'Just as the sun did rise' — morning explicit.

quotes.text:
When the wind whistles cold on the moor of a night,
Tom Pearce's old mare doth appear, gashly white.
quotes.source: Widdecombe Fair (Songs of the West, No. 16)
quotes.notes: Stanza 7, lines 1–2. Night explicit. Ghost of the old mare appearing on the moor.

quotes.text:
Come to my window, my Love, O my Love,
Come to my window, my Dear.
For my mammy is asleep,
And my daddy snoreth deep,
Then come, e'er the day-light appear.
quotes.source: Come to My Window (Songs of the West, No. 41)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1. Night implicit — before daylight, household asleep.

quotes.text:
Moon shining bright,
Dropping of dew.
Owls hoot all night
To-whit! to-whoo!
quotes.source: Lullabye (Songs of the West, No. 49)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 5–8. Night explicit.

quotes.text:
How comes it that thou stroll'st this way?
Sweet maiden, tell me true,
Before bright Phœbus' glittering ray
Has supped the morning dew?
quotes.source: Fair Lady, Pity Me (Songs of the West, No. 60)
quotes.notes: Stanza 2, lines 5–8. Morning implicit — before Phoebus (the sun) has drunk the dew.

quotes.text:
I sit by the cradle, my baby's asleep,
And rocking the cradle, I wonder and weep.
O little blue light,
In the dead of the night,
O prithee, O prithee no nearer to creep.
quotes.source: The Blue Flame (Songs of the West, No. 67)
quotes.notes: Stanza 2. Night explicit. The blue flame is a will-o'-the-wisp / soul of the dead.

quotes.text:
Why follow the church path, why steal you this way?
Why halt in your journey, on threshold why stay?
With flicker and flare,
Why dance up my stair!
O I would, O I would, it were dawning of day.
quotes.source: The Blue Flame (Songs of the West, No. 67)
quotes.notes: Stanza 3. Morning implicit — longing for dawn to end the haunting. Companion to ID 138.

quotes.text:
One summer evening, a maiden fair
Was walking forth in the balmy air,
She met a sailor upon the way.
quotes.source: The Hostess' Daughter (Songs of the West, No. 70)
quotes.notes: Stanza 1, lines 1–3. Evening and summer both explicit.
